---
title: "Tree-Based Methods"
subtitle: "BSAD 8310: Business Forecasting --- Lecture 9"
author: "BSAD 8310: Business Forecasting"
institute: "University of Nebraska at Omaha"
date: "Spring 2026"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, emory-clean.scss]
    slide-number: true
    show-slide-number: all
    transition: fade
    transition-speed: fast
    incremental: false
    toc: false
    center: false
    width: 1280
    height: 720
    margin: 0.05
    logo: ""
    footer: "BSAD 8310 | University of Nebraska at Omaha | Spring 2026"
    embed-resources: true
bibliography: ../Bibliography_base.bib
csl: https://www.zotero.org/styles/chicago-author-date
---

## Lecture 9: Outline

1. Decision Trees: CART
2. Bagging and Random Forests
3. Gradient Boosting and XGBoost
4. Feature Importance
5. Hyperparameter Tuning
6. Application to Forecasting

---

## Motivation: Beyond Linear Models

::: {.keybox}
**Lecture 08 best result:** Elastic Net, RMSE $\approx$ 2,540 on RSXFS.

Elastic Net is a linear model: $\hat{y} = \mathbf{x}^\top \hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}$. It cannot capture *interactions* or *threshold effects* (e.g., the holiday effect amplifies when the economy is strong).
:::

**What tree-based methods add:**

- **Nonlinearity:** predictions are piecewise constant over feature regions
- **Interactions:** splits on $x_1$ in a region defined by $x_2$ capture $x_1 \times x_2$ interactions automatically
- **No standardization required:** scale-invariant splits

**Today's goal:** build from a single decision tree to Random Forests and XGBoost, then compare against the Lecture 08 leaderboard.

---

# Decision Trees: CART

Building blocks of ensemble methods through recursive binary splits of the feature space

---

## Recursive Binary Splitting

**CART (Classification and Regression Trees) for regression:** at each node, find the split $(j, t)$ minimizing the weighted within-region variance:

$$\min_{j,\, t} \left[ \sum_{i:\, x_{ij} \leq t} (y_i - \bar{y}_{L})^2 + \sum_{i:\, x_{ij} > t} (y_i - \bar{y}_{R})^2 \right]$$

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="52%"}
**Algorithm:**

1. Start with all data at the root
2. For every feature $j$ and every threshold $t$: compute the split criterion
3. Split on the $(j^*, t^*)$ giving the largest reduction
4. Recurse on left and right children
5. Stop when nodes are too small or depth limit reached
6. Predict: $\hat{y} = \bar{y}_{\text{leaf}}$
:::

::: {.column width="44%"}
::: {.examplebox}
::: {.examplebox-title}
Why Recursive?
:::
Each split depends on which points fall into a region. Optimal splitting is NP-hard globally; greedy top-down splitting is the standard approximation (CART).
:::
:::

::::

---

## A Simple Regression Tree

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="44%"}
**Example: 3-leaf tree for RSXFS**

```{=html}
<svg viewBox="0 0 300 210" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" style="width:100%; max-height:190px; display:block; margin:0 auto; font-family:inherit;">
  <defs>
    <marker id="arr9" markerWidth="6" markerHeight="6" refX="3" refY="3" orient="auto">
      <path d="M0,0 L6,3 L0,6 Z" fill="#aaa"/>
    </marker>
  </defs>

  <!-- Root node -->
  <rect x="70" y="4" width="160" height="38" rx="5" ry="5" fill="#E8F0FA" stroke="#005CA9" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="150" y="20" font-size="11" fill="#1a1a1a" text-anchor="middle" font-weight="600">y(t-12) &lt;= 38,000?</text>
  <text x="150" y="35" font-size="9" fill="#525252" text-anchor="middle">(12-month lag)</text>

  <!-- Left leaf: pre-2010 -->
  <rect x="4" y="96" width="108" height="40" rx="5" ry="5" fill="#F0FAF4" stroke="#15803d" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="58" y="113" font-size="11" fill="#1a1a1a" text-anchor="middle" font-weight="600">y-hat = 28,400</text>
  <text x="58" y="128" font-size="9" fill="#525252" text-anchor="middle">(pre-2010)</text>

  <!-- Right internal node -->
  <rect x="158" y="88" width="136" height="38" rx="5" ry="5" fill="#E8F0FA" stroke="#005CA9" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="226" y="104" font-size="11" fill="#1a1a1a" text-anchor="middle" font-weight="600">y(t-1) &lt;= 52,000?</text>
  <text x="226" y="119" font-size="9" fill="#525252" text-anchor="middle">(1-month lag)</text>

  <!-- Left-right leaf: mid -->
  <rect x="128" y="166" width="106" height="38" rx="5" ry="5" fill="#F0FAF4" stroke="#15803d" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="181" y="183" font-size="11" fill="#1a1a1a" text-anchor="middle" font-weight="600">y-hat = 44,100</text>
  <text x="181" y="197" font-size="9" fill="#525252" text-anchor="middle">(mid period)</text>

  <!-- Right-right leaf: recent -->
  <rect x="246" y="166" width="50" height="38" rx="5" ry="5" fill="#F0FAF4" stroke="#15803d" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="271" y="183" font-size="10" fill="#1a1a1a" text-anchor="middle" font-weight="600">61,300</text>
  <text x="271" y="197" font-size="9" fill="#525252" text-anchor="middle">(recent)</text>

  <!-- Edges -->
  <line x1="120" y1="42" x2="58" y2="96" stroke="#aaa" stroke-width="1.2" marker-end="url(#arr9)"/>
  <line x1="180" y1="42" x2="226" y2="88" stroke="#aaa" stroke-width="1.2" marker-end="url(#arr9)"/>
  <line x1="200" y1="126" x2="181" y2="166" stroke="#aaa" stroke-width="1.2" marker-end="url(#arr9)"/>
  <line x1="252" y1="126" x2="271" y2="166" stroke="#aaa" stroke-width="1.2" marker-end="url(#arr9)"/>

  <!-- Edge labels -->
  <text x="80" y="74" font-size="9" fill="#525252" text-anchor="middle">Yes</text>
  <text x="210" y="68" font-size="9" fill="#525252" text-anchor="middle">No</text>
  <text x="186" y="150" font-size="9" fill="#525252" text-anchor="middle">Yes</text>
  <text x="260" y="150" font-size="9" fill="#525252" text-anchor="middle">No</text>
</svg>
```
:::

::: {.column width="52%"}
**Key properties:**

- **Non-parametric** --- no functional form assumed
- **Nonlinear** --- can capture threshold and interaction effects
- **Interpretable** --- decision path is readable
- **Handles mixed features** --- lags, rolling stats, dummies all treated equally
- **Scale-invariant** --- no need to standardize predictors

::: {.warningbox}
A single deep tree **overfits severely**. The solution is not pruning alone --- it is *ensembling*.
:::
:::

::::

---

## Overfitting and the Bias--Variance Tradeoff in Trees

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="52%"}
**Tree depth controls complexity:**

- **Depth 1** (stump): high bias, low variance
- **Depth 5--10**: balanced region
- **Full tree** (each leaf = 1 obs): zero bias, infinite variance

**Why trees have high variance:**
A single split can change the entire subtree beneath it. Small perturbations in data (e.g., one outlier) produce very different trees --- trees are *unstable*.

::: {.keybox}
**Key insight:** averaging many high-variance, low-bias trees (each fit to a bootstrap sample) substantially reduces variance while preserving low bias. This is bagging.
:::
:::

::: {.column width="44%"}

```{=html}
<svg viewBox="0 0 240 200" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" style="width:100%; max-height:200px; display:block; margin:0 auto; font-family:inherit;">
  <!-- Axes -->
  <line x1="30" y1="175" x2="225" y2="175" stroke="#1a1a1a" stroke-width="2"/>
  <polygon points="223,171 231,175 223,179" fill="#1a1a1a"/>
  <text x="234" y="179" font-size="11" fill="#1a1a1a">depth</text>
  <line x1="30" y1="180" x2="30" y2="10" stroke="#1a1a1a" stroke-width="2"/>
  <polygon points="26,12 30,4 34,12" fill="#1a1a1a"/>
  <text x="5" y="8" font-size="11" fill="#1a1a1a">error</text>

  <!-- Bias² curve: monotone decreasing (red) -->
  <path d="M35,20 C65,45 110,105 210,168" fill="none" stroke="#E41C38" stroke-width="2.5"/>
  <text x="46" y="38" font-size="10" fill="#E41C38">Bias²</text>

  <!-- Variance curve: monotone increasing (blue) -->
  <path d="M35,168 C80,158 130,118 210,28" fill="none" stroke="#005CA9" stroke-width="2.5"/>
  <text x="182" y="55" font-size="10" fill="#005CA9">Var</text>

  <!-- Total curve: U-shape (gray dashed) -->
  <path d="M35,26 C60,38 95,72 118,78 C140,82 165,102 210,160" fill="none" stroke="#525252" stroke-width="2" stroke-dasharray="6,3"/>
  <text x="168" y="120" font-size="10" fill="#525252">Total</text>

  <!-- Optimal depth marker -->
  <line x1="118" y1="175" x2="118" y2="78" stroke="#525252" stroke-width="1.2" stroke-dasharray="4,3"/>
  <text x="100" y="192" font-size="10" fill="#525252">opt. depth</text>
</svg>
```

:::

::::

---

# Bagging and Random Forests

Variance reduction through bootstrap aggregation and random feature decorrelation

---

## Bagging: Bootstrap Aggregating

::: {.definitionbox}
::: {.definitionbox-title}
Bagging
:::
Draw $B$ bootstrap samples; fit one deep tree on each; average:

$$\hat{y}_{\mathrm{bag}} = \tfrac{1}{B}\sum_{b=1}^{B} \hat{f}^{(b)}(\mathbf{x})$$

[@Breiman1996]
:::

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="52%"}
**Variance of the ensemble:**

$$\mathrm{Var}(\bar{f}) = \rho\sigma^2 + \tfrac{1-\rho}{B}\sigma^2$$

$\rho$ = pairwise prediction correlation; $\sigma^2$ = single-tree variance.

As $B \to \infty$, variance $\to \rho\sigma^2$ (not zero). Reducing $\rho$ matters as much as increasing $B$ --- this motivates Random Forests.
:::

::: {.column width="44%"}
::: {.keybox}
**Bagging key numbers:**

- Bootstrap sample $\approx 63\%$ unique obs.
- Remaining $\approx 37\%$ = out-of-bag (OOB)
- OOB obs. give a free cross-validation estimate
- $B \geq 500$ trees recommended (stable)
:::
:::

::::

---

## Random Forests: Decorrelating the Trees

**Problem with bagging:** if one feature dominates, all trees use it at the root $\Rightarrow$ trees are highly correlated ($\rho$ stays large).

**Random Forest fix:** at each split, randomly sample $m < p$ features and find the best split *among those $m$ only*.

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="52%"}
- Default: $m = \lfloor\sqrt{p}\rfloor$ (sklearn $\geq$ 1.1); $\lfloor p/3 \rfloor$ recommended for regression [@Hastie2009, §15.3]
- Reduces $\rho$ $\Rightarrow$ lower ensemble variance
- Each tree is weaker but the ensemble is stronger

::: {.keybox}
RF = Bagging + random feature selection at each split. Both ingredients are necessary.
:::
:::

::: {.column width="44%"}
::: {.examplebox}
::: {.examplebox-title}
Out-of-Bag Error
:::
Each obs. is OOB for $\approx 37\%$ of trees. Predict using only those trees; compute RMSE.

OOB RMSE $\approx$ 5-fold CV RMSE at large $B$ --- essentially *free* cross-validation.
:::
:::

::::

---

## Random Forests: Key Hyperparameters

::: {.smaller}

| **Parameter** | **sklearn name** | **Default** | **Effect** |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| Num. trees | `n_estimators` | 100 | More = lower variance; plateau after ~500 |
| Max features/split | `max_features` | `'sqrt'` | Lower = more decorrelated; tune via OOB |
| Max depth | `max_depth` | None | None = full tree; limit to reduce memory |
| Min samples/leaf | `min_samples_leaf` | 1 | Higher = smoother predictions; reduces overfit |
| Bootstrap | `bootstrap` | True | False = subsampling (not bootstrap) |

:::

[*Socratic: why does increasing `n_estimators` always reduce out-of-bag error, but increasing tree depth does not? (Hint: think about what each controls in the bias--variance decomposition.)*]{.neutral .smaller}

---

# Gradient Boosting and XGBoost

Sequential residual fitting with second-order optimization and explicit regularization

---

## Gradient Boosting: Sequential Residual Fitting

::: {.definitionbox}
::: {.definitionbox-title}
Gradient Boosting Algorithm
:::
Initialize $F_0(\mathbf{x}) = \bar{y}$. For $b = 1, \ldots, B$:

- Residuals: $r_i^{(b)} = y_i - F_{b-1}(\mathbf{x}_i)$ *(negative gradient for squared-error loss)*
- Fit shallow tree $T_b$ to $\{(\mathbf{x}_i,\, r_i^{(b)})\}$
- Update: $F_b = F_{b-1} + \eta\, T_b$

Predict: $\hat{y} = F_B(\mathbf{x})$
:::

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="54%"}
**RF vs. Boosting:**

- RF: **parallel** trees, deep, bias not reduced by adding more trees
- Boosting: **sequential**, shallow trees (depth 3--6)
- Boosting bias **decreases** with stages
- Both benefit from large $B$ (number of trees)
:::

::: {.column width="42%"}
::: {.warningbox}
Small $\eta$ + large $B$ + early stopping outperforms large $\eta$ + small $B$.

**Always use early stopping on a validation set.**
:::
:::

::::

---

## XGBoost: Three Advances Over GBM

XGBoost [@Chen2016] extends gradient boosting with three additions:

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="56%"}
- **1. Newton step (2nd-order):** Uses gradient *and* curvature of the loss to compute a more precise tree-fitting direction than first-order GBM.
- **2. Explicit regularization:** Penalizes leaf count and leaf weights directly in the objective --- shrinks trees without relying on early stopping alone.
- **3. Column subsampling:** Like RF, randomly samples features per tree *and* per split --- reduces correlation and overfitting.
:::

::: {.column width="40%"}
::: {.keybox}
Newton step $\Rightarrow$ more precise gradient direction.

Regularization $\Rightarrow$ guards against overfitting.

Together: XGBoost outperforms vanilla GBM on most benchmarks [@Chen2016].
:::

[*Mathematical formulation: next slide.*]{.neutral .smaller}
:::

::::

---

## XGBoost: Optimization Objective

*Here $f_b(\mathbf{x})$ is the output of tree $T_b$ in boosting step $b$ --- same tree, XGBoost notation.*

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="56%"}
**1. Newton-step objective (stage $b$):**

$$\mathcal{L}^{(b)} = \sum_{i} \bigl[g_i f_b(\mathbf{x}_i) + \tfrac{1}{2} h_i f_b^2(\mathbf{x}_i)\bigr] + \Omega(f_b)$$

$g_i$ = gradient, $h_i$ = Hessian of loss w.r.t. $F_{b-1}(\mathbf{x}_i)$.

**2. Regularization term:**

$$\Omega(f_b) = \gamma T + \tfrac{1}{2}\lambda \|\mathbf{w}\|_2^2$$

$T$ = leaf count, $\mathbf{w}$ = leaf weights, $\gamma$ = per-leaf complexity penalty.
:::

::: {.column width="40%"}
**Notation note:**

$\lambda$ here is XGBoost's L2 leaf-weight penalty. It is distinct from the regularization $\lambda$ (penalty strength) in Lecture 08.

**Practical effect:** Setting $\lambda > 0$ and $\gamma > 0$ produces sparser, more interpretable trees even without aggressive early stopping.
:::

::::

---

## XGBoost: Key Hyperparameters

::: {.smaller}

| **Parameter** | **XGB name** | **Typical** | **Effect** |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| Num. trees | `n_estimators`* | 500--2000 | More + small $\eta$ = better (use early stopping) |
| Learning rate | `learning_rate` | 0.01--0.1 | Smaller = more robust; requires more trees |
| Tree depth | `max_depth` | 3--6 | Shallow preferred; deeper = overfit risk |
| Subsample | `subsample` | 0.7--0.9 | Row fraction per tree; adds stochasticity |
| Col. sample | `colsample_bytree` | 0.7--0.9 | Feature fraction per tree (like RF) |
| L2 penalty | `reg_lambda` | 1 | Ridge on leaf weights; stabilizes |

:::

[*sklearn API name; native API uses `num_boost_round`.*]{.neutral .smaller}

::: {.keybox}
**Practical starting point:** `learning_rate=0.05`, `max_depth=4`, `n_estimators=1000` with early stopping on val RMSE. Tune `max_depth` and `subsample` via TimeSeriesSplit CV.
:::

[*Socratic: if `learning_rate` is halved, roughly how many additional trees are needed to maintain the same training loss? What does this imply for early stopping budget?*]{.neutral .smaller}

---

# Feature Importance

Impurity, permutation, and XGBoost gain measures for model interpretation

---

## Feature Importance: Impurity and Permutation

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="52%"}
::: {.definitionbox}
::: {.definitionbox-title}
1. Impurity-Based (RF Default)
:::

$$\operatorname{Imp}(j) = \sum_{\text{nodes using } j} \Delta\operatorname{RSS} \cdot \frac{n_{\text{node}}}{n}$$

Sum of RSS reduction at all splits on feature $j$, weighted by fraction of samples reaching that node.
:::

[*Caution: biased toward high-cardinality features (continuous variables get more split opportunities than binary dummies). Do not use for final reporting.*]{.neutral .smaller}
:::

::: {.column width="44%"}
::: {.definitionbox}
::: {.definitionbox-title}
2. Permutation Importance
:::
For each feature $j$:

1. Shuffle column $j$ in the OOB set
2. Record increase in RMSE

Larger increase $\Rightarrow$ more important.

**Unbiased** (no cardinality bias). **Model-agnostic.** Use for reporting.
:::
:::

::::

---

## Feature Importance: XGBoost Gain and Rankings

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
::: {.definitionbox}
::: {.definitionbox-title}
3. XGBoost Gain Importance
:::
Average *gain* (improvement in objective function) per split using feature $j$, across all trees.

More interpretable than impurity for boosted trees because it weights by actual objective reduction.
:::

**Practical rule:**

- Use **permutation** importance for model reports and presentations
- Use **impurity** for quick diagnostics during training
- Use **XGBoost gain** when comparing feature contributions inside a boosted model
:::

::: {.column width="46%"}
::: {.examplebox}
::: {.examplebox-title}
RSXFS Typical Rankings
:::

1. **lag_12** --- dominant seasonal signal
2. **lag_1** --- short-run momentum
3. **roll_mean_12** --- trend level
4. **lag_3** --- quarterly effect
5. **month_12** --- December holiday

Results are consistent across all three importance measures; lag_12 should be the primary feature in any RSXFS model.
:::
:::

::::

---

# Hyperparameter Tuning

Time-series-aware cross-validation with TimeSeriesSplit

---

## Tuning Random Forests and XGBoost

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="48%"}
**Random Forest (RandomizedSearchCV):**

```python
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestRegressor
from sklearn.model_selection import (
    TimeSeriesSplit, RandomizedSearchCV)

tscv = TimeSeriesSplit(n_splits=5, gap=1)
rf_grid = {
  'n_estimators':     [200, 500],
  'max_features':     ['sqrt', 0.33],
  'min_samples_leaf': [1, 3, 5],
  'max_depth':        [None, 10, 20],
}
rf = RandomizedSearchCV(
  RandomForestRegressor(random_state=42),
  rf_grid, n_iter=20,
  cv=tscv,
  scoring='neg_root_mean_squared_error',
  n_jobs=-1, random_state=42)
rf.fit(X_trainval, y_trainval)
```
:::

::: {.column width="48%"}
**XGBoost (early stopping on val):**

```python
import xgboost as xgb

dtrain = xgb.DMatrix(X_train, label=y_train)
dval   = xgb.DMatrix(X_val,   label=y_val)
dtest  = xgb.DMatrix(X_test,  label=y_test)

params = {
  'learning_rate': 0.05,
  'max_depth':     4,
  'subsample':     0.8,
  'colsample_bytree': 0.8,
  'reg_lambda':    1.0,
  'objective':     'reg:squarederror',
}
model = xgb.train(
  params, dtrain,
  num_boost_round=2000,
  evals=[(dval, 'val')],
  early_stopping_rounds=50)
```
:::

::::

---

# Application to Forecasting

Comparing Random Forest and XGBoost against SARIMA and Elastic Net on RSXFS

---

## Tree-Based Forecasting: Design Choices

**Same feature matrix as Lecture 08:** 12 lags + 3 rolling windows + 11 month dummies = 26 features (one dummy omitted vs. L08 for comparability; trees do not require it).

**Key differences vs. regularized regression:**

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
**Advantages of trees:**

- Capture nonlinearities and interactions (e.g., "holiday effect only when economy is strong")
- No standardization needed
- Handle irrelevant features gracefully (unused features do not appear in splits)
- Built-in feature importance
:::

::: {.column width="46%"}
**Cautions for time series:**

- **No extrapolation:** trees predict $\bar{y}_{\text{leaf}}$, so they cannot predict beyond the training range
- **Trending series:** a naive tree trained on non-stationary data may perform poorly out of sample
- **Fix:** use first-differences or add rolling statistics as features to encode trend
:::

::::

::: {.warningbox}
**Stationarity matters for trees too.** If $y_t$ trends upward beyond the training max, leaf means will systematically under-predict. Difference or detrend before applying tree-based forecasting.
:::

---

## Forecast Comparison: Adding Trees to the Leaderboard

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="52%"}
**Test-set results on RSXFS (24-month horizon):**

::: {.smaller}

| **Model** | **RMSE** | **MAE** |
|:---|---:|---:|
| Seasonal Naïve | 4,210 | 3,120 |
| SARIMA(1,1,1)(1,1,1)$_{12}$ | 2,840 | 2,100 |
| Elastic Net ($\lambda^*$) | 2,540 | 1,890 |
| Random Forest | 2,380 | 1,760 |
| XGBoost (early stop) | 2,250 | 1,650 |

:::

[*Values are illustrative. Strategy: direct multi-step --- one model trained per horizon $h = 1, \ldots, 24$.*]{.neutral .smaller}
:::

::: {.column width="44%"}
**Why do trees win here?**

- Nonlinear interaction between lag_12 and calendar dummies (holiday amplification)
- XGBoost sequential residual fitting corrects patterns that Elastic Net misses
- RF OOB error $\approx$ CV error at $B=500$

**Caveat:** gains depend heavily on feature quality. With a good feature set, Elastic Net and XGBoost are often competitive. Adding *more* features benefits trees more.

[*Socratic: XGBoost outperforms Elastic Net here. Does this guarantee trees will always win with more data? (Hint: consider the no-extrapolation warning.)*]{.neutral .smaller}
:::

::::

---

# Takeaways and References

What we learned and where to go next

---

## Lecture 09 Key Takeaways

::: {.keybox}
1. **Decision trees** partition feature space greedily; they are interpretable but unstable (high variance).
2. **Random Forests** (bagging + random features) reduce variance dramatically while keeping bias low. OOB error is a free CV estimate.
3. **Gradient boosting** reduces bias sequentially by fitting residuals. XGBoost adds L2 regularization and Newton optimization.
4. **Feature importance** helps identify which lags, rolling stats, and calendar features drive forecasts. Use permutation importance for unbiased estimates.
5. **Trees cannot extrapolate** --- ensure features encode trend and seasonality so predictions stay within the training distribution.
6. **RF vs. XGBoost:** prefer RF for speed and robustness with limited tuning; prefer XGBoost when maximum accuracy matters and you have time to tune `learning_rate` and `max_depth`.
:::

**Preview of Lecture 10:** Neural Networks --- LSTM and attention mechanisms can model long-range temporal dependencies that trees cannot.

---

## References {.smaller}

::: {#refs}
:::
